chunk_summary: Feeding safety in infants and young children with tachypnea or respiratory distress focuses on managing feeding risks, ensuring energy intake, and recognizing danger signs for urgent referral during illness and recovery.
learning_objectives: Understand feeding challenges in infants with respiratory distress and tachypnea.
Learn age-based fast breathing thresholds for triage and severity assessment.
Identify safe feeding practices during mild to moderate respiratory illness.
Recognize danger signs requiring urgent referral and facility-based care.
key_facts: Fast breathing thresholds: ≥50/min for 2–12 months, ≥40/min for 12–59 months (IMNCI algorithms).
Danger signs include chest indrawing, stridor in a calm child, cyanosis, grunting, or apnea.
For moderate tachypnea, offer smaller, frequent feeds or expressed breast milk via cup/spoon.
Severe tachypnea or choking during feeding requires urgent referral and possible NG feeding in facilities.
Saline drops and upright feeding can help manage nasal congestion during mild illness.
Caregivers should monitor reduced feeding, fewer wet diapers, increased breathing rate, or unusual sleepiness for same-day review.
reference_quote: Age-based fast breathing thresholds support triage (for example, 2–12 months ≥50/min and 12–59 months ≥40/min in IMNCI algorithms).
document_key_topics: Facility-based feeding protocols for severe illness
Caregiver education on danger signs and feeding adjustments
